coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of arginine-based carbon dots for highly selective detection of dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiaming
Zhang, Ruilin
Peng, Jun
Xu, Man
Hu, Zhiyuan
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: Arginine-based carbon dots (Arg-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method using arginine and urea as precursors. The as-prepared Arg-CDs displayed good aqueous solubility and typical excitation-dependent emission property, with the fluorescence quantum yield of 25.47 %. The fluorescence of Arg-CDs was gradually quenched by the addition of dopamine (DA) in the alkaline solution. However, under neutral and acidic condition, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs remained unchanged upon the addition of DA. The sensing mechanism was probably due to the rapid oxidation of DA to dopaquinone in alkaline condition, which could quench the fluorescence of Arg-CDs through the Inner Filter Effect (IFE). Moreover, the fluorescence change of Arg-CDs was proportional to the concentration of DA under the optimized condition. Because of the linear relationship, the detection limit of dopamine was calculated to be 0.28 μM with the detection range from 10 to 100 μM. The interference experiment was carried out to further verify the specificity of this fluorometric method. Finally, the fluorometry based on Arg-CDs has been successfully used to the determination of DA in human serum with high accuracy and satisfactory recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Internal filtration effect
Arginine
Dopamine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DA in real serum
Measurement of DA in real blood serum by Arg-CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: the size distribution of Arg-CDs); (b) HRTEM images; (c) XRD spectra; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of Arg-CDs (insets: images of Arg-CDs solution under daylight and excitation of 300 nm; (f) fluorescence spectra upon different excitation (from 280 nm to 360 nm).
Effects of (a) pH values on detection of DA; (b) concentrations of NaCl on detection of DA; (c) incubated time on the FI of Arg-CDs (200 μg/mL).

(a) The change of FL ratio (F0-F/F0) of Arg-CDs over the pH value range from 3 to 11. (b) The change of FL over the incubation time between 0 and 16 min. (c) Effects of the concentration of CDs on the FL between 25 mg/mL and 300 mg/mL.

(a) Calibration curve of the fluorescent signals of Arg-CDs versus the DA concentration (Insert is photographs of color change with different DA concentrations between 5 and 250 μM); (b) the linear relationship between fluorescent intensity and DA concentration; (c) effect of various substance on the PL intensity of Arg-CDs. (d) Effect of various substance on the PL intensity of Arg-CDs containing DA.

(a) a TCSPC of Arg-CDs and Arg-CDs-DA (375 nm excitation, delay time at 450 nm emission, pH = 11); (b) the zeta potentials of Arg-CDs and DA; (c) UV–vis spectrum of DA in the neutral condition (pH = 7) and in alkaline conditions (pH = 11); (d) UV–vis spectrum of DA in alkaline conditions (pH = 11) (gray); excitation spectra of Arg-CDs (red); fluorescence emission spectra of Arg-CDs (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different DA detection methods.
Determination of DA in human serums.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of arginine-based carbon dots for highly selective detection of dopamine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology
Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Arginine-based carbon dots (Arg-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method using arginine and urea as precursors. The as-prepared Arg-CDs displayed good aqueous solubility and typical excitation-dependent emission property, with the fluorescence quantum yield of 25.47 %. The fluorescence of Arg-CDs was gradually quenched by the addition of dopamine (DA) in the alkaline solution. However, under neutral and acidic condition, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs remained unchanged upon the addition of DA. The sensing mechanism was probably due to the rapid oxidation of DA to dopaquinone in alkaline condition, which could quench the fluorescence of Arg-CDs through the Inner Filter Effect (IFE). Moreover, the fluorescence change of Arg-CDs was proportional to the concentration of DA under the optimized condition. Because of the linear relationship, the detection limit of dopamine was calculated to be 0.28 μM with the detection range from 10 to 100 μM. The interference experiment was carried out to further verify the specificity of this fluorometric method. Finally, the fluorometry based on Arg-CDs has been successfully used to the determination of DA in human serum with high accuracy and satisfactory recovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) as a catecholamine neurotransmitter is essential for regulating learning, motivation, and movement [1]. The body will produce a series of clinical symptoms and signs upon the variation of dopamine content [2]. The previous study demonstrated that the morbidity of depressive disorder [3], schizophrenia [4] and Parkinson's disease [5] would increase if the body lacks dopamine, and the abnormal elevation of dopamine indicated the occurrence of diseases, such as pheochromocytoma [6], myocardial infarction, myasthenia gravis [7], etc. Thus, it's of incredible significance to build simple and efficient methods for dopamine detection. Up to now, there are many analytical techniques reported for the measurement of DA, such as chromatography [8], colorimetry [9], electrochemistry [10]. For electrochemical analysis, the detection of DA is susceptible to disturb by ascorbic acid [11] (AA) and uric acid [12] (UA) because of the close redox potentials. Therefore, the sensitivity of electrochemistry for DA detection is good, while the selectivity is not very satisfactory. As to chromatography, the operation is complex and time-consuming and requires expensive instruments. In contrast, the optical methods, especially fluorescent methods [13], featured as easy operation, low cost, quick response, and high sensitivity, which was widely used in the detection of DA.

Carbon dots (CDs) [14], is a novel carbon-based nanomaterials, which had attracted much attention by reason that their unique properties such as small size, great biocompatibility, low toxicity, remarkable water solubility, and low cost [15]. In addition, the excellent optical features of CDs broaden its application in many fields such as bioimaging [16], biological sensing [17], detection [18], photodynamic therapy [19] and photothermal therapy [20]. In general, the luminescent properties of CDs could be adjusted by the selection of precursors [15], solvents and reaction time during the preparation [21]. Therefore, CDs are expected to be an ideal alternative to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in fluorescent assays. To date, various CDs using different raw material as precursors have been developed to detect kinds of analytes, such as metal ions, enzymes, bacteria and disease markers [22–25]. In 2015, Weng et al. developed a fluorescent strategy for the determination of DA based on the formation of polydopamine on the surface of graphene quantum dots with detection limit as low as 8 nM [26]. In 2020, they reported another DA detection strategy based on acid phosphatase enzymatic oxidation DA to polydopamine [27]. These methods involved the polymerization of DA to polydopamine followed by the interaction with carbon dots to cause fluorescence quenching. Therefore, it's great of significance to establish a direct and simple fluorescence assay for the detection of DA with high selectivity and sensitivity.

In this context, herein, we established a fluorescent method for quantitative detection of DA with high accuracy and selectivity. In detail, the blue-emissive Arg-CDs were developed through one-pot hydrothermal method with readily available arginine and urea as precursors. The fluorescence of Arg-CDs can be significantly quenched by DA in alkaline condition (pH = 11). The sensing mechanism was probably attributed to the oxidation of DA to dopaquinone in alkaline conditions, which could quench and the fluorescence of Arg-CDs through IFE [28]. The fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs was proportional to the concentrations of DA with a linear correlation ranging from 10 to 100 μΜ. Based on this linear equation, the detection limit of DA was valued as 0.28 μΜ. The selectivity of Arg-CDs towards the detection of dopamine was evaluated with the general ions and biomolecules as interferences. Finally, the proposed fluorometry based on Arg-CDs was utilized to dopamine detection in real human serum samples, demonstrating the potential application in disease diagnosis and monitoring.

Arginase (600 mg), carbamide (228 mg), and deionized water (60 mL) were added into Teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction solution was cooled down to room temperature, the mixture solution was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 15 min) to discard insoluble and larger particles. Finally, the supernatant was dialyzed against water for 48 h followed by freeze-drying to obtain the carbon dots powder, which was stored at 4 °C for further testing and application. The synthetic process of Arg-CDs was shown in Scheme 1 .

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Arg-CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (with a quantum yield of 0.55) as a reference. The QY of Arg-CDs was obtained to the formula:
Φ =
Φ ′
k
k ′
η
η ′
2
where Φ means the QY of Arg-CDs; Φ′ means the QY of reference, k and k′ denote the slope corresponding to the integrated fluorescence area and absorbance value of Arg-CDs and quinine sulfate, η is the refractive index of the solvent.

The fluorescence detection of DA was conducted as follows: 200 μL of Arg-CDs solution (1.0 mg/mL) and 100 μL of DA with varied concentrations were mixed with 270 μL of buffer (pH = 11) solution. After mixing homogeneously and incubating for 15 min, the fluorescence intensity at 358 nm was measured (λex = 300 nm).

To investigate the specificity of Arg-CDs for DA detection, the interference experiment of general ions and biomolecules in DA samples were conducted. Specifically, the fluorescence signals of Arg-CDs were recorded upon the existing of various substances. The DA concentration was 1 mM, and the concentration of other substances was 5 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in real serum
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the human serum was precipitated through centrifugation at 3000 rpm for half an hour. The supernatant was then filtered by a 0.22 μm Millipore filter subsequent by dilution with deionized water for 20 times before use. Finally, DA with different concentrations was added to serum sample for the measurement of recovery.

The morphology and particle size of the synthesized Arg-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1a, the Arg-CDs were uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution with a spherical structure. Microstructures of Arg-CDs were observed through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in the Fig. 1b, Arg-CDs possessed the typical ordered lattices with an interspace of 0.21 nm, ascribed to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [29]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) curve of Arg-CDs exhibited a broad peak at 2θ = 21° (Fig. 1c), indicating an amorphous carbon phase [30]. The functional groups on the surface of Arg-CDs were determined through Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1d, a typical absorption peak at 3235 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH, and the signal at 2948 cm−1 ascribed to the CH stretching vibration. Besides, the characteristic bands at 1645, 1485, and 1341 cm−1 were corresponded to the CO stretching vibration, NH stretching vibration of primary amide, and NH stretching vibration of secondary amide, respectively. The above results showed that the surface of Arg-CDs was full of amino and amide groups.

The photophysical properties of Arg-CDs were observed by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1e, Arg-CDs exhibited strong absorption peak around 200 nm, which can be ascribed to the n → σ* electronic transition of the -NH2 domains. Under irradiation of 365 nm UV laser, the Arg-CDs solution emitted bright blue emission (inset in Fig. 1e). Moreover, Arg-CDs demonstrated the typical excitation-dependent fluorescence properties. The emission peak shifted from 350 to 450 nm upon the excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 360 nm (Fig. 1f). Subsequently, the functional groups and chemical composition of Arg-CDs were examined using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Fig. S1a showed that the full XPS spectrum of Arg-CDs presented three characteristic peaks at 284.75, 400.31 and 532.18 eV, which corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. S1b) exhibited three peaks at 284.7, 286.2, 288.5 eV, corresponding to CC, C-N/C=N and CO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectra of Arg-CDs (Fig. S1c) depicted two peaks at 398.6, 400.5 eV, which were attributed to CN and NH-C=O groups, respectively. The O1s spectra of Arg-CDs in Fig. S1d presented three peaks at 530.3, 531.7 and 533.2 eV, assigned to -OH, CO and CO groups respectively.

The fluorescence quantum yield of Arg-CDs was calculated with quinine sulfate as reference (fluorescence quantum yield in 0.05 M H2SO4 solution was 0.55). Based on the corresponding equation (Fig. S2), the QY of Arg-CDs was calculated to be 25.47 %. The fluorescence stability of Arg-CDs was evaluated under different pH value, NaCl concentration, and storage time. As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs did not exhibit any significant change in buffer solutions with different pH values. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs in different concentration of salt was similar with that in water (Fig. 2b), indicating the excellent property of resisting salt effect. After storage for more than 10 days (Fig. 2c), the fluorescence intensity of the Arg-CDs solution did not show any significant changes. All these results demonstrated the high colloidal stability of Arg-CDs and their potential application in biological imaging. To evaluate the stability of Arg-CDs under detecting condition, we recorded the FL intensity of Arg-CDs during 10 days of storage. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs did not show any significant change after storage for 10 days, indicating their high colloidal stability.

To achieve the optimum condition for the fluorometric detection, the influence of different factors on the detection of DA were investigated. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs solution in the presence of DA in the pH range of 3–11 was monitored. As indicated in Fig. 3a, the Arg-CDs almost stayed the same fluorescence intensity from pH 5 to pH 10 in the presence of DA, but decreased in the more basic conditions (pH = 11). Therefore, pH = 11 can be adopted for the subsequent detection of DA using the Arg-CDs solution. The second factor is incubation time between DA and Arg-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity decreased instantaneously upon addition of DA and reached a minimum in 10 min. Thus, 10 min of incubation time was used for further experiments to realize a more accurate detection effect. The last factor is the concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs enhanced gradually as the concentration (25–300 mg/mL).

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs upon various concentrations of DA (2–300 μM)) were measured. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence signals of Arg-CDs gradually grown with the concentration of DA. The fluorescence change (ΔF) displayed a good linear relationship with the concentration of DA over the range from 10 to 100 μM, with the correlation equation of ΔF = 0.00579[DA] +0.115 (R2 = 0.9964) (Fig. 4b). Based on this equation, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.28 μM (S/N = 3). Therefore, the proposed detection method had superiorities in detection limit compared to mostly previous reported materials (Table 1 ).

To study the specificity of this fluorometric method towards DA detection, the interfering compounds such as general ions and biomolecules existed in biological fluid samples were selected. As shown in Fig. 4c, the addition of other interferences such as UA, CA, Cys, Phe, GSH, Urea, Glu, Lac, Mal, Sue, Na+, K+ did not cause any significant decrease in the fluorescence of Arg-CDs, indicating their high selectivity towards DA. Moreover, the selectivity of Arg-CDs was also verified through the competition experiment. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the coexistence of other interferences has little affect the detection of DA. Therefore, the Arg-CDs can be acted as selective and efficient fluorescence sensor for DA detection without affecting by most competing interferences.

To clarify the detection mechanism of Arg-CDs towards DA, a series of experiments were conducted. Firstly, the fluorescence lifetimes of Arg-CDs and Arg-CDs-DA mixture were measured using fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence lifetime of the Arg-CDs-DA mixture was valued as 5.054 ns, which was similar with that of Arg-CDs (5.016 ns), indicating the fluorescence quenching of Arg-CDs by DA was probably ascribed to IFE or static quenching [36,37]. Second, comparing with that under neutral condition, the absorption spectrum of DA solution broadened and red-shifted at pH = 11, along with the appearance of a new absorption peak at 420 nm, probably ascribed to the oxidization of DA to dopaquinone under alkaline condition (Fig. 5c). Accordingly, the absorption spectrum of DA under alkaline condition greatly overlapped with the emission spectrum of Arg-CDs (Fig. 5d), indicating either Inter Filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) would happen. Furthermore, the zeta potential of DA and Arg-CDs was recorded. As shown in Fig. 5b, both DA and Arg-CDs exhibited negative charge with zeta potential of −14.7 and −19.7 mV, respectively. After incubation of DA with Arg-CDs solution for 15 min, the zeta potential of the resulting mixture was changed to −21.5 mV. Therefore, the negative zeta potentials would cause separation between two components through electrostatic repulsion, with distance greater than 10 nm [38]. According to the reported reference [39], a minimum distance of 10 nm between the fluorescence donor and the receptor was required for FRET. Thus, the quenching of Arg-CDs by DA could be ascribed to Inter Filter effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of DA in real blood serum by Arg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of this method for DA measurement in blood samples, the human serum spiked with known concentrations of DA (20, 30 and 40 μM) were utilized. The human blood serum samples were collected from health individuals at the local hospital. The content of DA was then calculated with the spiked recovery method. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates of the human serum varied between 98.25 and 102.52 %, with the RSDs varied between 1.8 and 3.1 %. These results demonstrated that the proposed detection method using fluorescent Arg-CDs had good reliability and excellent accuracy.

In brief, the blue-emissive Arg-CDs were fabricated through facile one-step method, which could be used as fluorescence sensor for selective detection of dopamine. The Arg-CDs demonstrated good water solubility, distinct fluorescence, and high stability. DA can be oxidized to dopaquinone in alkaline condition, which could quench the fluorescence of Arg-CDs through Inter Filter effect (IFE). The fluorescence intensity of Arg-CDs decreased in a dopamine-concentration-dependent manner, which was very suitable for the quantitative detection of DA. The high selectivity of Arg-CDs for DA detection was verified with common ions and biomolecules as interferences. Furthermore, the fluorescent method based on Arg-CDs was applied to the determination of DA in real sample with good reliability and high accuracy. Therefore, the as-prepared Arg-CDs had potential application in clinical diagnosis of dopamine related diseases.

Jiaming Chen: Writing – original draft, Validation. Ruilin Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun Peng: Supervision, Investigation. Man Xu: Validation, Conceptualization. Zhiyuan Hu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Fengshou Wu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601142), Opening Project of Chongqing Research Institute of WIT (grant no. Wgdcyy2023002), and the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (grant no. 2024BEEA06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for virus detection and therapy
